coredata.dc:description: Designing cost-effective and sustainable edible packaging films featuring multifunctional activities (antimicrobial, antioxidant, UV shielding) is of great interest in the field of food science and engineering. Herein, the films containing various components of silk sericin (SS), chitosan (CS) as well as carbon dots (CDs) were developed and their physicochemical, structural and bioactive properties were also investigated. Compared with the SS-made film alone, the bio-nanocomposite films (made up of SS, CS, as well as CDs) showed the enhanced flexibility, antimicrobial and antioxidant properties. Moreover, a good compatibility was present among these components, which were well dispersed in the film matrix network. Importantly, due to the existence of CDs, the bio-nanocomposite films indicated a blue fluorescent emission property, which could be used as an anti-counterfeit label. Lastly, the fruit-litchi was selected to verify the fresh-keeping effect of the bio-nanocomposite film, which is effective to reduce the loss of nutritional components, such as water, soluble solids, ascorbic acid, etc. The study can shed light on the use of SS as matrix and CDs as functional ingredient for facile and environmentally friendly preparation of the edible food packaging film as well as for its utilization in food preservation to treat fruits effectively.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Designing cost-effective and sustainable edible packaging films featuring multifunctional activities (antimicrobial, antioxidant, UV shielding) is of great interest in the field of food science and engineering. Herein, the films containing various components of silk sericin (SS), chitosan (CS) as well as carbon dots (CDs) were developed and their physicochemical, structural and bioactive properties were also investigated. Compared with the SS-made film alone, the bio-nanocomposite films (made up of SS, CS, as well as CDs) showed the enhanced flexibility, antimicrobial and antioxidant properties. Moreover, a good compatibility was present among these components, which were well dispersed in the film matrix network. Importantly, due to the existence of CDs, the bio-nanocomposite films indicated a blue fluorescent emission property, which could be used as an anti-counterfeit label. Lastly, the fruit-litchi was selected to verify the fresh-keeping effect of the bio-nanocomposite film, which is effective to reduce the loss of nutritional components, such as water, soluble solids, ascorbic acid, etc. The study can shed light on the use of SS as matrix and CDs as functional ingredient for facile and environmentally friendly preparation of the edible food packaging film as well as for its utilization in food preservation to treat fruits effectively.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to external factors, about one-third of the world's food produced is wasted every year (Trajkovska Petkoska, Daniloski, D'Cunha, Naumovski, & Broach, 2021). Therefore, in order to overcome this problem and produce high-quality food, people have actively developed advanced preservation and packaging technologies, and adopted effective methods to improve the quality and safety of food storage and transportation (Jafarzadeh et al., 2021; Panahirad et al., 2021; W.; Zhang, Cao, & Jiang, 2021). For a long time, plastic packaging has dominated the development of the packaging industry due to its good performance and convenience (M. Rangaraj et al., & Mittal, 2021; Mikkonen & Tenkanen, 2012). However, the non-biodegradability of plastic packaging and the hazards of chemical leaching to food are the main problems. With the enhanced consciousness of environmental protection, the edible biopolymer composite films have great potential in replacing plastic packaging (Jafarzadeh et al., 2021; Picchio et al., 2018; Sid, Mor, Kishore, & Sharanagat, 2021).

Natural polymers such as proteins, polysaccharides and lipids are often used as raw materials for biodegradable packaging materials. Moreover, these bio-based films or coatings are usually prepared by blending each other to enhance the comprehensive performances (M. Rangaraj et al., 2021; Mohamed, El-Sakhawy, & El-Sakhawy, 2020). Sericin is a water-soluble polymer protein from the silkworm. At present, sericin has been found to have a variety of biological functions. It has good reactivity and excellent biological activities such as biodegradability, biocompatibility, antibacterial, anti-oxidation and anti-ultraviolet, and is easy to absorb and release in water (Y.-Q. Zhang, 2002). For example, Masahiro et al. reported that the intake of sericin can increase the bioavailability of zinc, iron, magnesium and calcium in rats. And this shows the potential use of sericin as a functional food material (Sasaki, Yamada, & Kato, 2000). The silk treatment wastewater contains a large amount of sericin, which can be effectively recycled to avoid a large amount of waste of biological resources and is beneficial to the improvement of the ecological environment (H. Li, Shi, Wang, & Zhu, 2015). In order to overcome the shortcomings of single-component films, multiple polymers are usually combined through physical or chemical cross-linking to improve the performance of the film. Importantly, the film cross-linked, mixed or copolymerized with sericin is easy to carry out (Nam et al., 2021; Rangaraj, Devaraju, Rambabu, Banat, & Mittal, 2022). For example, Yun et al. reported a glycerin-plasticized sericin film, and their results showed that glycerin could effectively improve the mechanical properties (Yun et al., 2016). Among the polysaccharides, chitosan is one of the most easily available materials, and is the preferred candidate material for food preservation. Chitosan is a linear polysaccharide recognized for its nontoxicity, biodegradability, biocompatibility and many antibacterial properties. Moreover, it is commercially available and inexpensive. Chitosan is one of the excellent carriers of minerals, vitamins, and other functional substances (Rahman, Konwar, Majumdar, & Chowdhury, 2021). Because of these unique characteristics, chitosan has potential as packaging material for food preservation.

In recent years, nano-material composite films have been gradually developed and applied, because nano-materials can improve the performance of composite films. CDs are a new type of carbon nanomaterials with a diameter of less than 10 nm. Degummed silk is a biomass that contains a large amount of silk fibroin. It can be used as the precursor material for the preparation of CDs, and the obtained CDs have good fluorescence luminescence and antioxidant activity. Because of their simple preparation process and good biological activity, CDs were widely used in bio-imaging, catalysis, biomedicine and other fields (Kurian & Paul, 2021). For example, Lu et al. reported that CDs prepared with curcumin and citric acid had good broad-spectrum antibacterial activity and were of great significance for the treatment of infections (Lu et al., 2021). Some studies have reported that amine-passivated coarse carbon dots prepared by microwave-assisted hydrothermal method have excellent oxidation resistance, which can effectively prevent the oxidation of natural rubber (Huangfu et al., 2021). In addition, CDs have unique luminescent properties. They can emit fluorescence under UV lamp irradiation. A related study has been reported in which carbon dots were synthesized using oilseed press cake and used in the preparation of edible composite films. The fluorescent properties of CDs were used to prepare an anti-counterfeit packaging and the involvement of CDs improved the antioxidant activity of the edible film (Das Purkayastha et al., 2014). In summary, there are many reports on the application of CDs' antioxidant, antibacterial and fluorescent properties in other fields. However, most of the reported CDs with antioxidant properties are rarely used in food packaging materials, so further development and research are needed.

The aim of this work is to develop a new biodegradable food packaging material with good antibacterial, antioxidant and identifiable properties that can be used for the preservation of post-harvest litchi fruit.

Silkworm cocoon was purchased from Wei Xiaoya Silkworm Business Department, Liujiang District, Liuzhou City, Guangxi Province. Acid-soluble chitosan with a degree of deacetylation of 80.0%–95.0%, 2, 2’ - diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH, 96%), and glycerin were all purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China. Glacial acetic acid was purchased from Guangzhou Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd. 2, 20-azino-bis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) diammonium salt (ABTS, 98%) was purchased from U&V Holdings Sigma-Aldrich Co., Ltd. Deionized water (18.25 MΩ cm) was obtained from a Milli-Q system (Sichuan Youpu Ultra-pure Technology Co., Ltd, Sichuan, China). Escherichia coli (ATCC 8739) and Staphylococcus aureus (ATCC 6538) were provided by the Second Affiliated Hospital of Fujian Medical University (Fujian, China). All other reagents in this study were of analytical grade.

Sericin was extracted according to a previously described method with slight modification (Nam et al., 2021). The cocoons were cut into small pieces, and 10 g of fresh cocoon was mixed with 250 mL of distilled water. The solution containing silk fibers were subsequently boiled for 2 h at 100 °C. The above solution was filtered through filter paper to remove fibrin and then concentrated to 100 mL. Finally, the concentrate solution was frozen and dried to become powder or stored at low temperature for further preparation of composite films.

The CDs were prepared using cocoon silk as the precursor created by simple one-step hydrothermal process. Briefly, 1 g of the above degummed cocoon silk was mixed with 30 mL distilled water. Subsequently, the solution was transferred into the Teflon equipped stainless steel autoclave, and then placed in a drying oven followed by hydrothermal treatment at 220 °C for 24 h. The reaction solution was filtered to remove impurities and placed for 48 h. The solution was then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min and the supernatants were kept in fridge for further usage (Jin et al., 2015).

Silk sericin films were prepared following casting technique. Firstly, glycerol (1.2 g) was added to 100 mL solution of sericin, after being stirred for 10min at 50 °C was placed cross-linking 1 h. After drying, the SS film was obtained. Second, an equal amount of sericin solution was blended with chitosan solution at 25 °C under stirring for 30 min. Chitosan solution was prepared with 1.5% (w/v) Chitosan in 1% (v/v) acetic acid, stirred overnight at room temperature. The composite film obtained after drying was SS/CS film. Third, 20% CDs solution were added to the above solution and stirred for 30 min. The resulting composite film was labeled as SS/CS/CDs film. For each of the above film preparations, 20 mL of the film solution was dried in a Petri dish (diameter = 9 cm) at 37 °C to produce composite film.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of the carbon dots aqueous solution were obtained by an ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (METASH UV-9000S). The photoluminescence spectrum of CDs were performed using an FL970 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Techcomp, Shanghai China) with a quartzcell (1 mm) (Khedri et al., 2021). The morphology of the CDs were analyzed by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) on a Tecnai F30 electron microscopy with a voltage of 300 kV. The X-ray analysis was performed in sample of CDs using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD) (D8 Advanced) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å) at 40 kV and 40 mA. In continuous mode, the data were recorded over an angular range from 10° to 80° (2θ) in 10° steps for 1 min per step. Samples were recorded by using fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) (Thermo Scientific, USA) with a spectral range of 4000 to 400 cm−1. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on an Kratos Axis Ultra DLD spectrometer.

Test methods such as TTIR and XRD are the same as those in 2.5. The microstructure of the composite films was analyzed using scanning electron microscopy. The composite film was cut into small pieces, attached to conductive glue and sputtered with gold under vacuum conditions, and then scanned with an electron microscope (SEM) to obtain micrographs of the plane and cross-section of the composite film. The element distribution in the composite film was obtained by energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) scanning.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV blocking performance of the film
ce:section.ce:para: Ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometry was used to test the transmission spectrum of the bio-nanocomposite film at 200–900 nm. The ultraviolet–visible transmission spectrum was used to evaluate the ultraviolet shielding performance of the composite film.

The film thickness was evaluated using a hand-held micrometer with an accuracy of 0.001 mm. At least five random points of the films was measured and the average (mm) was expressed as the film thickness.

The contact angle of the film was measured with a contact angle meter (JY-PHb). The film sample was cut into blocks of 30 × 30 mm2 in size. Slowly by syringe dropwise deionized water to the film surface, the contact angle was recorded immediately and it was calculated using Adobe Acrobat 9.0 Professional software.

The measurement was carried out after slight modification according to the method described by Fu et al. (Fu et al., 2021). The WVTR values of samples were calculated using following the equation:
(1)

where, w is the weight loss (g) during the period of time t (h), A is the area of exposed sample (m2), and d is the thickness of the film (mm).

Mechanical tests were measured using a universal stretching machine (INSTRON 3369, America). The film samples were cut into 10 mm × 90 mm long strip and fixed tightly between the device grips, with initial separation distance set at 50 mm, which were pulled apart at a speed of 50 mm/min (X. Li et al., 2021). Tensile strength (TS) and elongation at break (EB) were calculated by using following formulae equation:

where, ΔL is the difference between the final length and the initial length (mm), and L0 is the initial length (mm) of film sample.

According to the method of Khedri et al., the disc diffusion method was used to evaluate the antibacterial activity of the sericin-rich composite film against Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli. The 100 μL (106 CFU/g) different bacteria were plated onto agar medium. The filter paper was soaked in the film solution for 2 min and placed on the inoculation medium. After incubation for 24 h at 37 °C, the inhibition zone size around the discs was measured (Khedri et al., 2021).

The activity of thin film solution to scavenge the DPPH+-radical was determined according to Zareie et al. with some adjustments. The DPPH solution (0.1 mM) was prepared in ethanol. Briefly, 0.5 mL of each sample was added to 3.5 mL of the DPPH solution. Blank control was prepared in the same method, but with substituting the sample by distilled water. Then, it was well shaken and transferred to a dark place and stored for 30 min (Zareie, Tabatabaei Yazdi, & Mortazavi, 2020). The absorbance was measured at 517 nm and the DPPH+-radical scavenging activity was obtained as follows:
(4)
DPPH scavenging activity
( % )
=
Acontrol - Asample
Acontrol
× 100
where, Acontrol and Asample are the measured absorbance of the control and sample, respectively.

The ABTS radical scavenging activity of the thin film solution was performed according to Fu et al. with slight modification. In brief, a solution (7 mM) of 2,2′-Azino-bis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) diammonium salt (ABTS) was prepared and mixed with potassium persulfate (2.45 mM). It was transferred to a dark place and stored for at least 16 h at room temperature to form the ABTS radical (ABTS·+). This stock solution was diluted with distilled water to achieve an absorbance of 0.70 ± 0.01 at 734 nm. Then, 100 μL of thin film solution as well as the control sample (100 μL distilled water) was added to 3.9 mL of the ABTS solution and kept in dark place for 20 min (Fu et al., 2022). The absorbance was read at 734 nm and the ABTS scavenging activity was obtained using Eq:
(5)
A B T S

where Acontrol and Asample are the measured absorbance of the control and sample, respectively.

The ability of samples to reduce iron was determined according to the method of Zheng, B et al. A volume of 0.5 ml of each sample was mixed with 1.25 mL potassium ferricyanide (1% w/v) and 1.25 mL of phosphate buffer (0.2 M, pH 6.6). The reaction mixtures were incubated for 20 min at 50 °C. After reaction, 1.5 ml of TCA (10%) was added. After centrifuging the mixture at 4000 rpm for 10 min and mixing its supernatant (1.25 mL) with distilled water (1.25 mL) and FeCl3 (0.1%; 250 μL) and stored at room temperature for 10 min. The absorbance of the resulting solutions was recorded at 700 nm (Zheng et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Edible composite film for litchi preservation
ce:section.ce:para: Litchi fruits were harvested at commercial maturity from a forest farm in Nan'an City, Fujian province of China and then transported to the laboratory for processing and storage. Healthy fruits without mechanical damage or infection were used in subsequent experiments. The fruits were cleaned, dried and divided into two groups randomly. One group of fruits was soaked in ultrapure water for 5 min as a control, and the other group was soaked in SS/CS/CDs film solution for 5 min. After drying, fruit were packed in polyethylene film bags (30 fruits per bag, 37 bags for each treatment). All the bags were stored for 6 days at ambient temperature (25 °C) and 85% relative humidity. During storage, 7 bags of fruits were randomly selected from each treatment group every day for evaluation; the color difference, Vc content, TA content and other indicators were measured. All indicator analyses were performed in triplicate (Jiang, Lin, Lin, et al., 2018).

The weight loss percentage of litchi fruits was recorded using the method of Liang et al. 90 fruits were randomly selected and divided into three groups of experiments, and the percentage of weight loss was calculated by comparing with the initial weight of the fruits (Liang et al., 2015). The fruit weight loss percentage was calculated by the following formula:
(6)
W e i g h t
l o s s
p e r c e n t a g e
( % )
=
M 1
−
M 0
M 0
× 100
where, M1 is the weight of the fruit on the day of sampling, and M0 is the weight of the fruit on the 0th day.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of titratable acidity, total soluble solids and vitamin C contents
ce:section.ce:para: The methods of Jiang et al. (2011) were used determine the titratable acidity (TA), total soluble solids (TSS), and vitamin C contents in the litchi pulp with some modifications. litchi pulp (10 g) from 10 fruits was homogenized and centrifuged for 20 min. The resulting supernatant were used to determine the TSS content using a manual refractometer (WYT-II) and the TA content was determined by titration with 0.1 M NaOH. Vitamin C content was determined using 10 fruits of litchi pulp (10 g).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of color characteristics of litchi fruit
ce:section.ce:para: 5 fruits were randomly selected, and the L* value, a* value, and b* value of 4 different parts of the equatorial surface of the outer epidermis of each litchi fruit were measured by a colorimeter, and the average value was taken. Three experiments were repeated for each treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the ability to scavenge DPPH free radicals
ce:section.ce:para: Litchi pericarp tissue (1 g) was homogenized in 8 mL of 80% ethanol solution at 4 °C. Then, the extracts were centrifuged for 20 min, and the supernatants were diluted with 80% ethanol solution to 10 mL. 1.5 mL sample extract was reacted with 1.5 mL of 60 μM/L ethanol solution. Finally, the ability to scavenge DPPH free radicals was assessed by monitoring the change in absorbance at 412 nm. The sample solution in the control group was replaced with an ethanol solution.

The MDA assay was slightly modified using the method of Ali et al. Carefully homogenize (1 g) in 8 mL of 10% TCA, and centrifuge at 12,000×g (4 °C) for 20 min. The supernatant (4 mL) was reacted with 0.67% thiobarbituric acid (4 mL), and heated and boiled for 20 min. After cooling, the OD values of the supernatant were measured at 600, 532 and 450 nm wavelengths. The control group replaced the sample with 10% TCA, and the results were expressed in nmol/g FW.

Analysis of variance was performed on the data and SPSS was used to evaluate the difference between each factor and each level, P < 0.05 indicates that there is a significant difference between the data. P < 0.01 indicates that the difference between the data is very significant. These diagrams were drawn using Origin2018 software.

CDs are widely used because of their special luminescence properties and strong antioxidant activity. The UV–visible spectrum of CDs (Fig. 1 a) shows an absorption peak in the ultraviolet region (275 nm), which is usually attributed to the π-π* transition of the C=C bond and the n-π* transition of the C=O bond (Singh, Bamrah, Khatri, & Bhardwaj, 2020). From the photoluminescence excitation (PLE) and photoluminescence spectra of CDs, it can be seen that under the excitation of 382 nm ultraviolet light, the strongest emission peak is concentrated at 445 nm. The inset in Fig. 1a shows that the CDs solution exhibits blue fluorescence under 365 nm ultraviolet light. When the excitation wavelength is changed from 370 nm to 450 nm, a small shift from 430 nm to 500 nm is observed (Fig. 1b). A slight shift in the fluorescence peak indicates a narrower particle size distribution or a smaller number of energy paths that result in emission (Gaikwad, Joshi, Patil, Sathaye, & Rode, 2019). As shown in Fig. 1e, the three-dimensional excitation and emission spectra show that CDs can exhibit blue light under ultraviolet light excitation (370 nm–450 nm).

The XRD pattern of CDs (Fig. 1c) has a narrow diffraction peak at a 2θ value of 20.79°, which corresponds to a graphite structure (Tang et al., 2012). In order to determine the functional groups of CDs, XPS and FTIR spectral data were analyzed and plotted in the graph. The FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1d) shows that there are typical stretching vibrations of amine groups and hydroxyl groups between 2800 and 3150 cm−1 (Nxele & Nyokong, 2022; Song et al., 2021). The signal peak here is weak, indicating that the process of synthesizing carbon dots by silk has been effectively carbonized. The tensile vibration at 1587 cm−1 and the vibration at 1514 cm−1 are matched with the carbon-based (C=O) vibration and C=C, respectively (Ahmed et al., 2021; Gabashvili, Menikh, Ségui, & Fragata, 1998). In addition, the stretching vibration corresponding to C–N at the strip at 1346-1459 cm−1, and the vibration peak between 950 and 1120 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of C–O (Song et al., 2021). The results show that the surface of the carbon dots contains a wealth of functional groups. These functional groups make the carbon dots have good solubility and facilitate the formation of hydrogen bonds with sericin during film preparation. The full-scan XPS spectrum of the prepared CDs (Fig. 2 a) shows that the three main peaks are located at 284, 399, and 532 eV, corresponding to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively. In addition, the C 1s (Fig. 2b) consists mainly of peaks at 285.3 eV, 286.5 eV, and 288.7 eV, attributed to C=C, C–N, and C–O, respectively. the N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2c) shows two characteristic peaks at 400.0 eV and 400.7 eV, which correspond to the C=N–C, N-(C)3, and N–H relative nitrogen species, respectively. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 2d) shows two characteristic peaks at 531.7 eV and 532.6 eV, which correspond to the C=O and C–OH/C–O–C bonds, respectively(Lim, Liu, Kim, & Son, 2018). In addition, the morphology and particle size of CDs were studied by HR-TEM. As shown in Fig. 1f, it can be clearly seen that CDs are composed of nanoparticles with an average particle size of 1.76 nm. The carbon nano-particles obtained by hydrothermal synthesis of silk have good dispersibility. The fluorescence intensity of CDs under different pH conditions was measured, as shown in Fig. S5. When the pH value increased from 3 to 12, the fluorescence intensity of CDs gradually weakened.

Fig. 3 shows the microstructure of the composite film. The film surface (Figs. 3a and b) is smooth. The cross-sectional image (Fig. 3c) also shows that the film is smooth, crack-free, and dense in structure. This may be due to the relatively small volume of CDs and their ability to occupy the pores of the porous membrane matrix. The reduction of micropores helps to improve the barrier properties of the silk film. The EDS test results show that the film mainly contains elements such as C, N, and O, which are uniformly distributed in the film. This also proves that the CDs is evenly fused into the film.

As shown in Fig. 4 b, the sericin film exhibited characteristic peaks of the diffraction pattern at 2θ of 19.2°. It is assigned to the crystalline phase of sericin. After adding chitosan and carbon dots into the sericin film, the characteristic diffraction peaks of sericin shifted. As shown in Fig. 4a, the broad peaks at 3200–3600 cm−1 for the sericin film correspond to O–H and N–H stretching vibrations (He et al., 2017). The peaks at 1619 cm−1, 1519 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 correspond to amide I, amide II and amide III peaks, respectively (Oh et al., 2021). The peak corresponding to 1045 cm−1 may be the C–O stretching and C–O–H bending vibrations of amino acids in sericin (Laity, Gilks, & Holland, 2015). It can be seen from the spectra that the peak positions and peak sizes of the SS/CS and SS/CS/CDs composite films have changed. All the above results indicate that blending can promote the formation of intermolecular or intramolecular hydrogen bond networks between components.

In order to understand the UV barrier properties of the composite film, judge by the transmittance (%) of the composite film in the ultraviolet and visible light regions. As shown in Fig. 4c, the pure sericin film can block ultraviolet light in the UV-C (230–280 nm) region, and the transmittance of the visible light region is relatively low. The film after blending and crosslinking of sericin and chitosan cannot absorb ultraviolet light, but the light transmittance in the visible light region is significantly improved. The composite film of sericin, chitosan and carbon dots can completely absorb the ultraviolet light in the UV-C (200–280 nm) and UV-B (280–315 nm) regions (Barman, Sele Handegård, Hashimoto, & Nagao, 2021). The UV-A (315–400 nm) region also absorbs part of the ultraviolet light, and the transmittance in the visible light region is higher, which can reach 80%. It shows that the composite film has good UV blocking ability without compromising the transparency of the visible light region. Therefore, the composite film can be used for food packaging to prevent photooxidation. The above results show that the composite film can be used for food packaging, does not affect the appearance of the food and the value of the product, and has the function of anti-counterfeiting.

The composite film used for food packaging is usually in contact with the outside world, and it is unavoidable to avoid contact with water in the environment. Sericin, chitosan and CDs are all hydrophilic substances, so the composite film is relatively fragile in water. The water contact angles of the SS film, SS/CS film and SS/CS/CDs film were 55.83°, 68.38° and 68.75°, respectively (Table 1 ), all of which were hydrophilic. According to the image of the scanning electron microscope, it can be seen that the film structure is relatively compact and dense. And with the increase of the components, the thickness of the film increases. Adding different ingredients does not affect the thickness of the film. Although the surface of the film is hydrophilic, it is difficult for water to penetrate the inside of the film in general, and its hydrophilicity can be overcome, (Das Purkayastha et al., 2014). Figs. S1 and S2 also supports this view. In addition, Fig. S3 shows the edible laminate film showing blue fluorescence under UV light. The results in Fig. S6 demonstrate the safety of the composite film as an edible film.

In order to reduce the brittleness of the sericin film, glycerin was added as a plasticizer during the preparation of the composite film, and the tensile strength and elongation at break of the composite film were measured. The results showed that the tensile strength and elongation of the different components of the film were in the range of 1.23–11.63 MPa and 18.53%–54.71%, respectively. The tensile strength of SS/CS film can reach 11.6 MPa, and the elongation at break is about 24%. The tensile strength of the film added with CDs is reduced, but the elongation at break is significantly increased. Probably because of the hydrophilicity of CDs, the increase in the water content of the film leads to a decrease in the tensile strength. In addition, CDs may combine with sericin and glycerol to form hydrogen bonds, protein-protein interactions are reduced, and polymer segment mobility increases, so the elongation at break is enhanced (Das Purkayastha et al., 2014).

Many foods are often spoiled due to bacterial contamination, which affects their quality. The agar disc diffusion method was used to evaluate the antibacterial effects of films containing different components against Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli. As shown in Fig. 5 , a pure sericin film solution was used as a control. SS/CS film solution and SS/CS/CDs film solution have antibacterial effects on Staphylococcus aureus (Gram-positive bacteria) and Escherichia coli (Gram-negative bacteria). The results showed that the addition of CDs increased the inhibition zone of the film. Fig. S4 also shows that CDs have antibacterial activity against Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus. This may be because bacteria are micro-scale organisms, while the pores in bacterial films are nano-scale. Therefore, small CDs can permeate the bacterial cell wall, causing the leakage of intracellular components of the bacteria and their death. And the SS/CS/CDs film has better therapeutic effect on Escherichia coli than on Staphylococcus aureus, which may be due to the difference in the structure of the bacterial cell outer membrane (Varghese & Balachandran, 2021). Applying the composite film to the preservation of fruits can prevent bacterial contamination.

According to previous reports, sericin has certain anti-oxidation ability. Its antioxidant capacity is provided by the hydroxyl groups of serine and threonine, delocalized electrons of aromatic acids, and polyphenols (Jena et al., 2018). But the antioxidant activity of sericin needs to be improved. Therefore, CDs were added to prepare a composite film with stronger antioxidant activity. As shown in Figs. 4d, 4e and 4f, the scavenging ability of the film solution to DPPH and ABTS free radicals and the reducing power were measured. The results show that the film solution without CDs has a certain degree of free radical scavenging ability and reduction ability. The addition of CDs significantly improved the free radical scavenging activity and reducing ability of the composite film solution. The antioxidant activity of CDs increased gradually.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of color characteristics of litchi fruit
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 6 , the skin color of litchi fruit gradually darkened due to browning during storage. Browning began to occur on the second day of storage, and the browning was severe on the fourth day. In contrast, the browning of the litchi fruits treated with the film was weaker than that of the control group. The skin color of the litchi fruit is very important to the commercial value of the fruit. In general, the L*, a* and b* values of all fruits decreased gradually throughout storage regardless of treatment (Wang et al., 2020). The L* value represents brightness. Negative and positive a* values indicate green and red, respectively, and negative and positive b* values indicate blue and yellow. The results in Fig. 7 show that the L*, a* and b* values of litchi fruits show a gradual decline during storage. During storage, the L*, a* and b* values of fruits treated with SS/CS/CDs composite film solution were higher than those of untreated fruits. These results indicate that the composite film solution treatment can maintain the color and brightness of the litchi peel and reduce the browning of the peel.

The increase in the percentage of weight loss of litchi fruits is mainly due to the loss of water (Su et al., 2019). As shown in Fig. 8 a, with the prolongation of storage time, the weight loss of litchi fruits in the control group and the SS/CS/CDs composite film treatment group continued to increase. During the storage period, the weight loss of litchi fruits in the SS/CS/CDs film treatment group was significantly lower than that in the control group. Therefore, we believe that the composite film treatment can inhibit water transpiration by maintaining the integrity of the cuticle of litchi fruit, thereby reducing the weight loss of litchi fruit and maintaining the quality of litchi fruit.

The content of TSS in fruit can also be used as a sign of fruit ripening. It can be seen from Fig. 8b that the soluble solid content in the litchi pulp gradually decreases during storage. The TSS content decreased rapidly from the third day, and the soluble solid content of litchi fruits treated with SS/CS/CDs film solution was higher than that of the untreated group. Significance analysis results showed that the soluble solid content of the treated fruits was significantly higher than that of the untreated group on the 6th day of storage. Generally speaking, on storage, TSS is expected to increase over time. The increase in TSS content in the fruit eventually leads to fruit spoilage. The increase in TSS during fruit storage may be due to the substantial loss of fruit moisture, in which the sugar concentration may increase (Pandey et al., 2013).

As shown in Fig. 8c, during the storage period, the acidity of the control group and the SS/CS/CDs composite film treatment group gradually decreased during the storage process, and the composite film treatment could significantly inhibit the decrease of TA content. The decrease in TA content was mainly due to the action of organic acids, which are an important source of general metabolism after fruit harvest, including the respiration tricarboxylic acid cycle (Jiang, Lin, Shi, et al., 2018).

In Fig. 8d, the ascorbic acid content of the treated and untreated fruits gradually decreased. Although there was no significant difference, the ascorbic acid content of litchi fruits treated with the composite film solution was always higher than that of the untreated group. The above results indicate that the composite film solution can prevent the loss of ascorbic acid. It is beneficial to the retention of nutrients in litchi fruit or to slow down its loss during storage. The above results all show that the SS/CS/CDs film solution treatment can effectively delay the deterioration of the fruit and increase the commercial value of the fruit.

DPPH free radical scavenging activity and reducing power are important parameters to evaluate the overall non-enzymatic antioxidant activity. As shown in Fig. 9 a, the DPPH free radical scavenging ability of litchi fruits in the control group and the film-treated group showed a decreasing trend during the whole storage process. The composite film treatment significantly suppressed the decrease in the DPPH radical scavenging rate during the storage period of 2–5 days, which is consistent with the reported findings (Z. Zhang et al., 2015). The decreased non-enzymatic antioxidant activity of litchi peel may be related to the degradation of anthocyanins.

As shown in Fig. 9b. During the storage period, the MDA content of litchi pericarp in the control and treatment groups showed a gradual upward trend, and the SS/CS/CDs composite film treatment group always maintained a low level. Practical SS/CS/CDs composite film treatment of post-harvest litchi fruit significantly inhibited the production and accumulation of MDA content compared to the control group. The content of 1–3 day and 5 day was significantly different. Oxidative stress can lead to cell film damage and increase MDA content. Film-preservation may protect cell membranes by maintaining antioxidant activity and reducing fruit senescence, thereby reducing the increase in MDA content (Ali et al., 2019).

In conclusion, CDs were prepared by a simple one-step hydrothermal method using natural silk, which has anti-ultraviolet, anti-oxidative, good biocompatibility and easy interaction with substrates. CDs were added to sericin (extracted from waste) and chitosan to prepare the edible bio-nanocomposite film with anti-counterfeiting, antibacterial, antioxidant, UV shielding, and biocompatibility. More importantly, edible bio-nanocomposite films are used for food preservation (post-harvest litchi preservation), which can help delay the loss of nutrients during storage and maintain the better quality of the litchi fruit. Silk protein and CDs combined with some natural biological macromolecules may be a promising "green" precursor for the preparation of environmentally friendly edible multifunctional food packaging films.

Shanshan Mei: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Bofei Fu: Formal analysis, Investigation. Xianjie Su: Formal analysis, Investigation. Hongbin Chen: Formal analysis, Investigation. Hetong Lin: Supervision. Zongping Zheng: Writing – review & editing. Congjie Dai: Writing – review & editing. Da-Peng Yang: Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.
